prep.notes: watch.py exited 0 (detail=balanced). 100 frames in scratchpad/confirm/Q10_srZ-pbs/control/frames/. Report at control_report.txt. Ground-truth transcript (36300 chars) copied unchanged to eval/transcript.txt.

ctlAnswers.answer:
SUMMARY: This is a Veritasium video (the "Ve" channel logo appears on the end card alongside sponsor Brilliant, on-screen at [32:31]) telling the ~300-year story of the principle of least action, framed as "a single simple rule that underpins all of physics" from classical mechanics and electromagnetism to quantum theory, general relativity, and fundamental particles [00:00]-[00:16]. The opening frames visually reinforce this: a cartoon scientist stands at a chalkboard covered in equations from across physics, including Maxwell's equations (∇×B = μ0(J + ε0 ∂E/∂t), ∇·B = 0), E = mc², and a Schrödinger-like ħ²/2m ∇²ψ term (on-screen at [00:03]-[00:16]). The host even suggests it may explain "the behavior of life itself" [00:30]-[00:33], with b-roll of ants and synchronized metronomes as illustration.

The story: (1) The brachistochrone problem, what ramp shape slides a mass from A to B fastest [00:51]-[01:03]; a straight A-to-B line is shown on screen [01:10] but a curved ramp that gains speed early wins [01:12]-[01:21]. Galileo claimed the arc of a circle [01:32]-[01:36], but this conflicts with the eventual answer: the true solution is a cycloid, not a circle [09:46]-[09:53], so Galileo was wrong. Johann Bernoulli posed it as a challenge in 1696 [01:43]-[01:49], largely to beat Newton [02:12]-[02:19]; Newton solved it in one night by 4 a.m. [02:30]-[02:57], prompting Bernoulli's line "I recognize the lion by his claw" [03:15]. (2) Bernoulli's more beautiful solution [03:31]-[03:42] built on optics: Hero of Alexandria said light takes the shortest path [04:01]-[04:04], but refraction breaks that [04:18]-[04:26] (Snell's law, [05:01]); Fermat showed in 1657 that light instead minimizes TIME, deriving Snell's law from it [05:38]-[06:22], with the on-screen formula sin θi/v1 = sin θr/v2 and a travel-time-vs-position curve whose minimum marks the actual path (shown around [04:47]-[06:41]). This was the first demonstration that "nature obeys an optimization principle" [07:06]-[07:10]. Bernoulli converted the sliding-mass problem into an optics problem [07:26]-[07:31] and found the brachistochrone is a cycloid [09:28]-[09:53], also the tautochrone ("same time") curve [10:05]. (3) In the 1740s Maupertuis proposed a deeper minimized quantity, the "action" = mass × velocity × distance [11:07]-[11:12] (a cartoon shows him writing "Action" with a quill, on-screen near [11:10]), illustrated with a 0.5 kg ball: 9 + 9 = 18 units of action over two segments [11:44]-[12:01]. He was ridiculed (Voltaire wrote a 32-page pamphlet mocking him) [12:32]-[12:56], partly justifiably, since the principle was picked "out of thin air" and lacked rigor [13:40]-[14:01]. (4) Euler defended it, replaced the sum with an integral, and found it requires conserved, path-identical energy [14:09]-[15:20]; Lagrange, at ~24, provided a general proof [16:30]-[16:37]. (5) The video then walks a three-step derivation (an on-screen list reads "Three step approach: 1. General approach 2. Modern form 3. Why it works", shown near [17:08]-[17:19]): the least-action path is found like a function's minimum, where the first-order variation is zero (an on-screen parabola with df/dx = 0 illustrates this, near [17:37]-[17:40]) [17:30]-[19:15]; Maupertuis' action is rewritten into Hamilton's Principle, the variation of ∫(T − V)dt = 0 (on-screen: δ∫mvds = 0 transforming into δ∫(T−V)dt = 0 labeled "Hamilton's Principle", near [21:18]-[22:16]), named for William Rowan Hamilton, 1834 [22:07]-[22:13]; and applying it to a thrown ball yields exactly F = ma [23:25]-[26:07].

THE PRINCIPLE AND WHY IT MATTERS: The principle of least action states that of all conceivable paths between two points (with fixed endpoints and times, in Hamilton's form [22:55]-[23:03]), nature follows the one for which the action, the integral of kinetic minus potential energy over time [21:23]-[21:31], is stationary: "The variation of the action is 0" [26:43], shown on-screen as δS = 0 circled on a chalkboard (frame image matching this passage). It matters because: (a) it is equivalent to Newton's Second Law yet "covers more than just mechanics" [26:04]-[26:09]; Fermat's least time is just a special case [26:14]-[26:18], so reflection, refraction, pendulums, planetary orbits, and stars orbiting the galaxy all unify "under a single simple rule" [26:21]-[26:39]; (b) practically, the Euler-Lagrange machinery lets you solve any mechanics problem by writing down kinetic and potential energy and plugging in, "and you're done" [28:01]-[28:10], working in any coordinate system [28:46]-[29:01] and taming problems that are brutal with forces, like the double pendulum [29:04]-[29:29] (a physical double-pendulum rig appears on screen); (c) it points beyond classical physics: action was key to solving the UV catastrophe (an on-screen blackbody-radiation graph shows classical theory diverging in the ultraviolet, near [30:31]) and "starts the ball rolling toward quantum theory" [30:34]-[30:42]; a follow-up video is promised [30:49]-[30:53]. One caveat the video itself flags: the name is misleading, the action need not be a true minimum, only stationary [29:37]-[30:14]. The video closes with a knowledge-compounds message and a Brilliant sponsorship segment [31:02]-[32:41]. The narration is by host Derek with two interviewed experts, one identified in the transcript as Steven [08:01] (shown via webcam in many frames); a second, younger interviewee appears in frames but is never named in the transcript, so his identity cannot be determined from this evidence.

No, the principle of least action is NOT always a true minimum, and the video says the more accurate name is the PRINCIPLE OF STATIONARY ACTION.

The video addresses this directly in a dedicated "side note": the narrator says "there is one little side note we should give about the principle of least action, because the name is a little misleading" [29:33]-[29:39]. The interviewed physicist then explains: "Although we often refer to the principle as the principle of least action... it's probably good to add a little caveat here, that sometimes it's not necessarily the minimum" [29:40]-[29:52]. He draws the analogy to ordinary calculus: "Just as when you find in calculus when you set a derivative to 0, that doesn't guarantee you're getting the minimum of a function" [29:52]-[29:58]. Earlier in the video, this derivative-equals-zero idea is shown on screen as a parabola f(x) with a horizontal line labeled df/dx = 0 at its bottom (on-screen graphic accompanying [17:37]-[17:44]).

The corrected name is stated explicitly: "The principle of least action more properly stated should be the principle of stationary action. That the laws of motion come from demanding a stationary point, which is tantamount to this condition of setting a certain derivative to 0 and then getting the Euler-Lagrange equation from that" [29:58]-[30:13]. He concludes: "So very often it is a true minimum, but not always" [30:14].

Reconciling this with the rest of the video: throughout, the video uses the traditional name "principle of least action" and language about nature minimizing quantities, e.g., Maupertuis claiming the followed path "is the one that minimizes the action" [12:07]-[12:14], and the unifying rule being written as "the variation of the action is 0" [26:43] (shown on-screen as δS = 0 circled on a chalkboard in a frame matching this passage). There is no contradiction: the mathematical condition actually used everywhere in the video is the stationarity condition, that the first-order variation of the action vanishes ([19:07]-[19:23]: "the change in action between the optimal path and some trial path is 0" to first order; on-screen formulas near [21:18]-[22:16] show δ∫mvds = 0 and δ∫(T−V)dt = 0, the latter labeled "Hamilton's Principle"). That condition identifies a stationary point, which is usually, but not always, a true minimum, exactly as the caveat at [29:49]-[30:14] states. The video does not give a concrete example of a non-minimum (e.g., saddle-point) case, so no such example can be cited from this evidence.

evAnswers.answer:
Summary: This is a ~32-minute Veritasium-style physics history video (hosted by Derek, with physicist interviewees) about the principle of least action, "a single simple rule that underpins all of physics, every principle, from classical mechanics to electromagnetism, from quantum theory to general relativity" [00:00]-[00:19]. The opening frame at [00:10] shows an animated hand chalking equations on a blackboard, and an interviewee admits "we're approaching spooky territory" [00:26] and fears the local differential-equation picture of the universe may be "exactly backwards" [00:34]-[00:46].

The story unfolds historically:
1) The brachistochrone (fastest descent) problem: what ramp shape slides a mass from A to B fastest [00:55]-[01:03]? On-screen frames show a curved A-to-B ramp [01:01] and a family of candidate paths from A to B [01:32]. Galileo guessed a circular arc [01:32]; in 1696 Johann Bernoulli (portrait shown on screen at [03:34]) posed it as a challenge [01:43], Newton solved it overnight [02:47]-[02:57], but Bernoulli's solution was the most beautiful [03:31].
2) Optics detour: Hero of Alexandria showed light in one medium takes the shortest path [04:01], but refraction breaks that [04:25]. Snell's Law (sin of incidence over sin of refraction = constant n) emerged [04:47]-[05:01], and in 1657+ Fermat explained it via the principle of least time: light takes the path of shortest total travel time [05:38]-[06:22]. An on-screen demo frame at [06:38] shows a laser refracting through a block with angles theta_i and theta_r, a travel-time-vs-position graph with a minimum, and the formulas sin(theta_i)/sin(theta_r) = n and n = v1/v2. Fermat's was "the first time... anyone shows that nature obeys an optimization principle" [07:04]-[07:10].
3) Bernoulli's solution: he converted the mechanics problem into optics [07:26], modeling the falling mass as light speeding up through layered media, with v proportional to sqrt(y) (shown as a split-screen "v ∝ √y" diagram at [08:47]), yielding a cycloid, the brachistochrone curve (the frame at [09:43] shows a rolling wheel tracing the labeled "Brachistochrone curve") [09:28]-[09:43].
4) Maupertuis' principle: in the 1740s Maupertuis (depicted in animation at [10:48] and [12:38]) proposed that nature minimizes a quantity he called the action = mass x velocity x distance [11:07]-[11:12], illustrated with a 0.5 kg ball example (the "0.5 kg" ball is on screen at [11:45]) totaling 18 units of action [11:44]-[12:01]; "This action is the true expense of Nature, which she manages to make as small as possible" [12:19]. He was attacked, ridiculed, and ignored (Konig and Voltaire accusations) [12:32]-[13:31], partly justifiably since he picked the principle "out of thin air" and it wasn't rigorous [13:40]-[13:59]. A manuscript frame at [13:48] shows "Action, S₀ = Σmvs" with "the farther / the faster / the more massive → more action".
5) Euler and Lagrange: Euler replaced the sum with an integral [14:09] (the on-screen math evolves from "Σmvs" at [14:49] to "S₀ = ∫mvds" with an orbit around a central mass M at [15:51]), found the principle requires conserved, path-equal energy [15:03]-[15:12], and young Lagrange delivered a general proof around a year after Maupertuis' death [16:30]-[16:34]. Derek (on screen at [14:11]) and the interviewee (e.g., [16:52]) narrate this.
6) The general variational approach: like finding a function's minimum by setting the derivative to 0 [17:30]-[17:40] (a f(x)-vs-x curve is on screen at [17:32], and a trial path from A to B at [17:54]); perturb the least-action path by a tiny function eta and the action doesn't change to first order [18:01]-[19:20] (the frame at [18:55] shows a parabola with the perturbation η at its minimum and "df/dx = 0"). Compactly: the variation of the action is 0, shown on screen as "δS₀ = 0, S₀ = Σmvs" at [19:42].
7) Modern form: transforming Maupertuis' action ∫mv ds into ∫(T−V)dt, kinetic minus potential energy integrated over time [19:48]-[21:31] (derivation steps like "δ∫(T−V)dt + Eδt = 0" are on screen at [20:58], and both forms "δ∫mvds = 0" and "δ∫(T−V)dt = 0" at [21:59]). Hamilton first wrote it this way in 1834, so it's called Hamilton's Principle [22:07]-[22:16]; a "Differences" chart on screen at [23:00] contrasts Maupertuis' Principle (path, integral over s, same energy) with Hamilton's Principle (trajectory, integral over t, same time) [22:49]-[23:13].
8) Why it works: applying it to a thrown ball (Derek holds a ball outdoors at [23:27]; the height-vs-time trajectory plot with y1, y2, t1, t2 is on screen at [24:02]; the variational integral δS = ∫(m dy/dt dη/dt − ηV'(y))dt = 0 at [25:03]) shows the least-action path satisfies exactly F = ma [24:00]-[25:57]: "the principle of least action is equivalent to Newton's Second Law" [26:04].
9) Practical power: the Lagrangian L = T − V and the Euler-Lagrange equation let you solve any mechanics problem from energies alone [27:44]-[28:10], even in weird coordinates and for nasty systems like the double pendulum [28:45]-[29:29]. A caveat notes the name is misleading: the action is not always a true minimum, so it is more properly the principle of stationary action [29:37]-[30:14].
10) Quantum teaser: around 1900 (period street footage on screen at [30:29]), action was key to solving the UV catastrophe, starting quantum theory, "Not energy and not force, action" [30:23]-[30:42], with more promised in a future video [30:49]. It closes with a message about compounding knowledge [31:02]-[31:10] and a Brilliant sponsorship [31:24]-[32:41].

Why it matters: the principle unifies what were entirely separate fields, light reflecting and refracting, pendulum clocks, planetary and galactic orbits, under one rule, "the variation of the action is 0" [26:21]-[26:43]; Fermat's least time is just a special case [26:14]; it is equivalent to Newton's Second Law but more general [26:04]-[26:09]; it makes hard problems tractable ("you don't have to be a good physicist" with the Lagrangian machine [28:21]-[28:29]); and it turns out to be the doorway to quantum theory [30:23]-[30:42].

No, the principle of least action is not always a true minimum, and the more accurate name, per the video, is the principle of stationary action.

The video addresses this directly in a side note: "there is one little side note we should give about the principle of least action, because the name is a little misleading" [29:33]-[29:37]. The physicist interviewee then explains: "Although we often refer to the principle as the principle of least action, it's probably good to add a little caveat here, that sometimes it's not necessarily the minimum" [29:40]-[29:49]. The reason given is the calculus analogy: "Just as when you find in calculus when you set a derivative to 0, that doesn't guarantee you're getting the minimum of a function" [29:52]-[29:57]. The corrected name is stated explicitly: "The principle of least action more properly stated should be the principle of stationary action" [29:58]-[30:01]. The laws of motion "come from demanding a stationary point, which is tantamount to this condition of setting a certain derivative to 0 and then getting the Euler-Lagrange equation from that" [30:03]-[30:11]. The bottom line: "So very often it is a true minimum, but not always" [30:14].

This is consistent with how the condition is actually formulated mathematically earlier in the video: the working statement is that the variation of the action between the true path and a slightly perturbed trial path is zero to first order [19:15]-[19:20], not that the action is smallest. On-screen content supports this: the frame at [19:42] displays the equations "δS₀ = 0" and "S₀ = Σmvs" (a stationarity condition, delta-action equals zero, rather than a minimization statement), and the frame at [17:32] shows a graph of a function f(x) versus x used for the find-the-minimum-by-zero-derivative analogy [17:30]-[17:40].

Reconciling an apparent conflict in the evidence: through most of the video the principle is described in minimization language, e.g., Maupertuis claimed the ball follows "the one that minimizes the action" [12:13] and quotes Nature managing the action "as small as possible" [12:19]; the general approach asks for "the one with the least action" [17:27]; and the ball example concludes "the action is minimal for the path that satisfies this curious differential equation" [25:31]. The video itself resolves this tension in the [29:33]-[30:14] caveat: the everyday name and the minimization framing are historically entrenched and usually correct in practice ("very often it is a true minimum" [30:14]), but the mathematically accurate statement is that the action is stationary (its first-order variation is zero), which only sometimes coincides with a true minimum, just as a zero derivative in calculus can indicate something other than a minimum [29:52]-[30:11].

per_question.rationale: Both cover all 4 gold facts: unification of physics [00:00]-[00:19]/[26:21]-[26:43], action as integral of kinetic minus potential energy over time [21:23]-[21:31] (B states it crisply; A via its item 7), stationarity ('variation of the action is 0' plus the [29:37]-[30:14] caveat), and the classical-mechanics/optics/quantum connection (Fermat least time as special case [26:14], F=ma equivalence [26:04], UV catastrophe/quantum [30:23]-[30:42]; Feynman paths are absent from this transcript so neither can cover that parenthetical). Spot-checked >=3 citations per answer, all accurate: A's [01:32] Galileo arc, [22:07]-[22:16] Hamilton 1834, [15:03]-[15:12] Euler energy conditions, [16:30]-[16:34] Lagrange timing; B's [10:05] tautochrone, [12:32]-[12:56] Voltaire pamphlet, [08:01] Steven label, [02:12]-[02:19] Newton target. No claim in either is contradicted; visual claims in both are plausible. Completeness offsets: A uniquely has v proportional to sqrt(y), Konig, the 'true expense of Nature' quote, and the full Maupertuis-vs-Hamilton differences chart; B uniquely has the tautochrone, 'lion by his claw', Newton's 4 a.m., Lagrange's age, and the Steven identification. Neither dominates; both lose one point only for the handful of minor omissions relative to the other. Tie at 9.

Both answer correctly: not always a true minimum, more accurately the principle of stationary action. Both cover all 3 gold facts: (1) 'No, not always a minimum' with the direct quote 'sometimes it's not necessarily the minimum' [29:40]-[29:49] and 'very often it is a true minimum, but not always' [30:14]; the transcript never says 'saddle point', and neither answer fabricates one (B explicitly and honestly notes no concrete non-minimum example is given); (2) the corrected name via the verbatim [29:58]-[30:01] quote; (3) the historical naming, A calls the least-action name 'historically entrenched' anchored to Maupertuis' minimization quotes [12:13]/[12:19], B calls it 'the traditional name' anchored to [12:07]-[12:14]; neither names Fermat here, a marginal shortfall shared equally. Spot-checked citations all verify: the [29:33]-[30:14] caveat block, A's first-order-variation citation [19:15]-[19:20] and [25:31] minimal-action line, B's [19:07]-[19:23] and the Hamilton's Principle formulas near [21:18]-[22:16]. Both reconcile the video's minimization language with the stationarity condition using the same calculus analogy [29:52]-[29:57]. Quality, accuracy, and citation fidelity are effectively identical; a point off each for omitting the Fermat/least-time half of the historical-naming fact. Tie at 9.

Both answers are comprehensive, chronologically faithful, and cover all 4 gold facts: unification of physics (A cites [26:21]-[26:43], B cites [26:21]-[26:39]), action as integral of T-V over time (A [19:48]-[21:31], B [21:23]-[21:31]), stationarity of nature's path (both cite the [29:37]-[30:14] caveat and 'variation of the action is 0' [26:43]), and the classical-optics-quantum connection (both cover F=ma equivalence, Fermat's least time as special case, and the UV-catastrophe quantum teaser; Feynman paths are not in the video so neither can be penalized for omitting them). Spot-checked 5+ citations per answer, all accurate. Differentiators cancel out: A hedges better ('Veritasium-style' vs B asserting the end-card logo; A's '1657+' vs B's flat 'showed in 1657' when the transcript says Fermat solved it ~5 years later, lines 139-144) and covers the Maupertuis-vs-Hamilton differences chart; B gives a crisper dedicated statement of the principle (fixed endpoints/times, integral of T-V, stationary), adds the tautochrone [10:05], correctly names Steven [08:01], and honestly flags the unnamed second interviewee, but carries more unverifiable visual specifics (Maxwell equations, ants/metronomes). Both minor flaws: A calls the interviewee a 'physicist' though he self-identifies as 'a math guy' (line 733). Net quality is equal.

Both answer correctly: No, not always a true minimum; the accurate name is the principle of stationary action; and both reconcile the historical minimization naming via Maupertuis' claims [12:07]-[12:19] and the entrenched traditional name. All spot-checked citations verified verbatim ([29:40]-[29:49] caveat, [29:52]-[29:58] calculus analogy, [29:58]-[30:14] stationary-action statement and 'very often it is a true minimum, but not always'). Gold fact 1's saddle-point detail is not stated in the video ('not necessarily the minimum' is all the source gives), so both are credited for the core 'not always a minimum'; B name-drops 'saddle-point' but only as an example it explicitly says cannot be cited from the evidence, an honest touch that roughly offsets A's more thorough reconciliation of every in-video minimization phrasing ([12:13], [12:19], [17:27], [25:31], all verified). Neither mentions Fermat by name in the targeted answer for the historical-naming fact, but both convey the naming-is-historical point via Maupertuis and the traditional-name framing, so fact 3 is credited to both. B's 'interviewed physicist' label conflicts slightly with the speaker's 'as a math guy' self-description (line 733), matching A's identical flaw. Quality is indistinguishable.

Both cover all 4 gold facts (unification, action = integral of T-V over time, stationarity of nature's path, classical/optics/quantum connection) with dense, spot-check-accurate citations ([00:00]-[00:19], [11:07]-[11:12], [16:30]-[16:34], [22:07]-[22:16] all verified for A; [02:12]-[02:19], [12:52]-[12:56], [21:23]-[21:31] verified for B). A has no material misstatement. B has two blemishes: it dates Fermat's least-time derivation to 1657 when the transcript says he solved it five years after getting interested ([06:15]-[06:19]), and claims a 'physical double-pendulum rig' on screen where the transcript says 'this is actually how we made this simulation' [29:29]-[29:33]. B also leans harder on unverifiable visual specifics (exact Maxwell equations on the chalkboard, ants/metronomes b-roll), plausible but speculative. A edges it on accuracy.

Both answer correctly and completely: no, the action is not always a true minimum, and the accurate name is the principle of stationary action. Both quote the caveat block verbatim and accurately ([29:40]-[29:49], [29:58]-[30:01], [30:14] all verified), both cover the historical entrenchment of the minimization naming via Maupertuis, and both correctly reconcile with the video's earlier minimization language (A cites [12:13], [17:27], [25:31], all verified; B cites [12:07]-[12:14], [19:07]-[19:23], verified). Neither invents a saddle-point example the transcript lacks; B explicitly and honestly flags that absence, while A adds slightly richer reconciliation cites. Neither names Fermat in the historical-naming point, a shared minor gap. Quality is indistinguishable.
